content.full_text:
Re:
Fnrn Por-rrrcAt- Pnecrrce s CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramenro, CA g5gl4-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 14,2Ol0
Ronald D. Packard
Four Main Street, Suite 200
Los Altos, CA94022
Your Request for Advice
Our file No.I-09-254
Dear Mr. Packard
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Because your qu-estion seeks general
guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 Nothing in this
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already takenplace, and
any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition,
this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(197s) I FPPC ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
Do you have a conflict of interest that would prevent you from participating in
your capacity as City Councilmember for the City of Los Altos in "visioning workshops"
organized by the city council with the general purpose of ascertaining the public's views
on growth and related issues for the city's downtown?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through l8gg7 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Infbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Secrion 83 I I4: Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. l-09-254
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
At this time, we do not have enough information to determine whether you will
have a conflict of interest under the Act as there are no clear govemmental decisions
before you and we cannot ascertain the effects those decisions may have on her economic
interests. To assist you as the "visioning workshops" proce.s *ou", forward, we provide
below a preliminary partial analysis of your question. We also include our brochure,
"Can I Vote" to aid you in the future.
FACTS
You are a city councilmember for the City of Los Altos. Los Altos is currently
considering changes to the city in light of the recent economic downtum. To that end, the
city council is hiring a consultant and developing "Visioning Workshops" that will
consist of two city councilmembers, city staff, citizens, and the consultant. After the
workshops are completed, the participants will present their results and recommendations
to the city council during open session. The workshops will be a precursor to possible
changes in the general plan for downtown Los Altos. You own an 18,000 square foot
office building in downtown Los Altos that you lease to several different buiinesses,
including your own.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regularion 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-(g).)
Step One: Are you a.,public official', under the Act?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to all "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bxl).) A "public official" is .,every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or Iocal government agency . . ..,, (Section g204g.) As
members of the city council, the individuals are public officials within the meaning of the
Act.r
3 If a public official's office is listed in Section 872W (*8720}filers" include members of a
county board of supervisors) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public
meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each
type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic inteiest, as discussed
in Regulation 18702.5(b)( I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave
the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars,


File No. l-09-254
Page No. 3
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Regulation L8702.2.) A public official is attempting to uie hiJor her official
position to influence a decision if, for the puqpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3(a).) A public official is also attempting to use his or her position to
influence a governmental decision when the official, for the purpose of influencing a
decision by another agency, act or purports to act on his or her agency's behalfbefore an
official of the other agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).)
. As you have described the "visioning workshop," the group will be making
recommendations to the city council and the council will then decide whether to accept or
reject the recommendations. The participants in the workshops will be presenting thelr
ideas and opinions to the city council. At that point, the city council wiil determine
whether they want to take the necessary steps to follow the recommendations of the
workshop participants.
The recommendations made to the city council will be participating in
governmental decisions that the city council will make regarding changes to Los Altos as
a city. You will therefore be participating in govemmental decision und"r Regulation
18702.2.
Step Three: What are your economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the ofiicial's economic
interests. The applicable economic interests include:
1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(a),
Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in any business entity in
which a public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87 103(d), Regulation I 8703. t(b).)
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules tbund in
Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section g7105.)


File No. I-09-254
Page No.4
2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b),
Regulation 18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the
public official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 18703.3.)
4. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of
his or her immediate family. This is also known as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.)
You have an interest in your real property located in downtown Los Altos. We
assume you have at least a $2,000 investment in this property.
You also have an interest in the sources of income from this property, that is, the
lease amounts garnered from the tenants in the property. Because you share ownership
with your two brothers, the appropriate inquiry would be the amount of lt3 of the lease
amounts collected. Additionally, you have an economic interest in your law firm as a
source of income.
You also have an economic interest in your personal finances and those of your
immediate family. As stated in Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a
personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing."
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
You have not given us enough facts to determine whether the potential decisions
and recommendations will have a direct or indirect effect on your interests. For your
information, the applicable standards for your stated economic interests are:
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part, that real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if "[t]he real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part ofthat real property, is located within 500 feet ofthe boundaries (or the proposed


File No. l-09-254
Page No. 5
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision
indirectly involved.
..4 -^.
' It lt ts not, lt ls
Sources of Income
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or indirectly by
an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official,s agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the isiuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation
18704.1(a).)
In all other cases, the source of income is indirectly involved. (Regulation
18704(a).)
P e rs onal financ ial effect s
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
A govemmental decision will have an effect on this economic interestlf the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)s
We do not have enough information to determine whether your economic
interests are directly or indirectly involved. Nor do have enough information to fully
analyze the remaining steps. As the advisory commission moves forward with its
recommendations, we encourage you to both consult the enclosed brochure ("Can I
Vote?") and to contact us again should you have a particular issue with which we can
assist you.
a "If a public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is
indirectly involved." (Regulation 1870a(a).)
5 A financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value ofassets and liabilities ofa business
entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect investment interest, are not considered separate
financial effects on the official's personal finances and would not be analyzed separately under the
"personal t'inancial effects" rule. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the perional t'inancial effects rule
does not appear to apply to your circumstances.


File No. l-09-254
Page No. 6
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322_5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
(hrilli^ il houlq,,Y
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure
